revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宇佐八幡宮神託事件]]
{{Wikisource|和気清麻呂為勅使参宇佐宮事被書絵詞|qq=}} '''Usa Hachimangū Oracle Incident''' (宇佐八幡宮神託事件, ''Usa Hachimangū Shintaku Jiken'') refers to a political and religious conflict during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} in the third year of [[Jingo Keiun]] (769 CE). The incident involved an oracle from [[Usa Jingū|Usa Hachimangū]], which purportedly decreed that {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|qid=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō|1=Dōkyō}}, a powerful Buddhist monk, should ascend to the throne of Japan, leading to significant political strife. The dispute, also known as the '''Dōkyō Incident''' (道鏡事件), was resolved on {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|qid=Q2913|lt=October 1|1=October 1}} (Lunisolar: 10th month, 1st day), when [[:en:Empress Shōtoku|Empress Shōtoku]] issued an edict declaring that Dōkyō would not succeed to the throne. == Course of Events == === Dōkyō's Political Ascension === {{ill|Dōkyō|en|Dōkyō|qid=Q1272718|lt=Dōkyō|1=Dōkyō}} gained Empress [[Kōken]]'s (later Empress Shōtoku) trust after healing her illness. Following the {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|en|Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|qid=Q11623017|lt=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion|1=Fujiwara no Nakamaro Rebellion}} (764 CE), which Empress Kōken quashed with support from discontented nobles, she deposed {{ill|Emperor Junnin|en|Emperor Junnin|qid=Q329713|lt=Emperor Junnin|1=Emperor Junnin}} and declared herself emperor once more in [[Jingo Keiun]] (reinstating her reign). Dōkyō rose in Rank and influence, becoming ''Daijō-daijin'' in 765 CE and ''Hōō'' (法王, Buddhist King) in 766 CE, wielding considerable power in the court. Despite Empress Shōtoku's favor, many nobles who supported the rebellion against Nakamaro opposed her reinstatement and Dōkyō's rising influence. With no children or clear successor, the issue of imperial succession became increasingly critical. The court saw several purges, including the exile of Emperor Junnin, execution of Prince Wake, and the stripping of royal status from Princess Fuwa. Succession discussions became a taboo subject in court politics. === Two Oracles === * '''First Oracle:''' In the fifth month of Jingo Keiun 3 (769 CE), Dōkyō’s brother [[Yuge no Jōnin]] and [[Nakatomi no Habuto Asomaro]], a senior official at {{ill|Dazaifu|en|Dazaifu|qid=Q1179277|lt=Dazaifu|1=Dazaifu}}, conveyed an oracle from Usa Hachimangū stating that "if Dōkyō ascends the throne, the nation will know peace." Upon hearing this, Dōkyō expressed his desire for the throne.<ref>''Shoku Nihongi'', Record of the Incident.</ref> The Empress sought confirmation and initially planned to send Hōkin (法均, [[Wake no Hiroimushi]]) to investigate, but due to his frail health, her brother {{ill|Wake no Kiyomaro|en|Wake no Kiyomaro|qid=Q7961005|lt=Wake no Kiyomaro|1=Wake no Kiyomaro}} was dispatched instead. * '''Second Oracle:''' At Usa Hachimangū, [[Ōgami no Tamaro]], the chief priest, delivered a divine message. Wake no Kiyomaro read a decree in the shrine, but the shrine's priestess, [[Karashima no Yosome]], refused to accept it. When Kiyomaro questioned her again, Yosome invoked a divine revelation, and a deity appeared, stating, "The imperial lineage has been established since the beginning of this nation. Never shall a subject be emperor. The imperial heir must come from the imperial bloodline." This oracle was brought back to the court. Empress Shōtoku, reportedly angered by the report, demoted Kiyomaro to ''Inaba-no-Gun'' official and exiled him to {{ill|Ōsumi Province|en|Ōsumi Province|qid=Q981002|lt=Ōsumi Province|1=Ōsumi Province}}. His sister Hiroimushi was also exiled and renamed ''Wakabe no Kitamaro'' (別部穢麻呂) as part of their punishment.<ref>The renaming reflected the era's belief in the spiritual power of words ([[kotodama]]), making it a meaningful punishment.</ref> On {{ill|October 1|en|October 1|qid=Q2913|lt=October 1|1=October 1}}, an imperial edict warned against pursuing the throne recklessly and emphasized that imperial succession would remain the Empress's decision. === Resolution of the Incident === Following Empress Shōtoku's death in the first year of {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|qid=Q18520676|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki}} (770 CE), [[Shoku Nihongi]] records that court deliberations led to [[Shirakabe no Ō]] (later {{ill|Emperor Kōnin|en|Emperor Kōnin|qid=Q958662|lt=Emperor Kōnin|1=Emperor Kōnin}}) becoming emperor. Dōkyō was exiled to {{ill|Shimotsuke Province|en|Shimotsuke Province|qid=Q1053699|lt=Shimotsuke Province|1=Shimotsuke Province}} and lived the rest of his life at [[Yakushiji Temple]]. Though a proclamation accused Dōkyō of aspiring to the throne, no clear evidence of his actions was presented. == Interpretations == The incident has often been viewed as a critical threat to Japan's imperial lineage. [[Shoku Nihongi]] portrays Wake no Kiyomaro as a loyal subject and Dōkyō as a political schemer, making the event a cornerstone of historical education in pre-war Japan. However, modern scholars, including {{ill|Motoori Norinaga|en|Motoori Norinaga|qid=Q384647|lt=Motoori Norinaga|1=Motoori Norinaga}}, have questioned the veracity of this narrative, suggesting it was fabricated to legitimize Emperor Kōnin's ascension. === Doubts on Dōkyō’s Intentions === * '''Yugi Palace Relocation Theory:''' Historian [[Yasuyuki Nakanishi]] argues that the oracle initially referred to relocating the capital to Yugi Palace, Dōkyō’s hometown. He contends that the accusation of treason against Dōkyō was retroactively applied by political factions, including the [[Fujiwara clan]]. Wake no Kiyomaro’s role was part of a fabricated narrative to promote succession for [[Prince Shirakabe]] or [[:en:Prince Ōtomo|Prince Ōtomo]]. * Critics of this theory note the lack of evidence supporting relocation as the oracle’s subject. Additionally, political outcomes following the incident align with the Fujiwara’s shifting power dynamics. === Empress Shōtoku’s Role === Scholar [[Hiroshi Hosoi]] suggests Empress Shōtoku orchestrated the incident to assert her authority over imperial succession. By invoking the oracle, she sought to designate her preferred heir while deflecting criticism. Hosoi interprets Dōkyō’s exile and the subsequent selection of Shirakabe as strategic moves to consolidate power. == Legacy == The incident connects [[:en:Yao City|Yao City]] (Dōkyō’s birthplace), [[Wake Town]] (Kiyomaro’s hometown), and [[Usa City]] (site of Usa Hachimangū), which now maintain sister-city relationships. == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * Nakanishi, Yasuyuki. ''Zoku Nihongi to Nara Politics''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2002. ISBN 978-4-642-02382-5. * Hosoi, Hiroshi. ''Ancient Astronomical Events and Historical Chronicles''. Yoshikawa Kōbunkan, 2007. ISBN 978-4-642-02462-4. == Related Articles == * {{ill|Adachiyama|ja|足立山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Adachiyama|qq=}} * [[Adachi Myokengu]] * {{ill|Nihon Kōki|en|Nihon Kōki|qid=Q1077029|lt=Nihon Kōki|1=Nihon Kōki}} {{Japanese-history-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|宇佐八幡宮神託事件|version=103076460|comment=Imported full ja history}}
